title: Celtic Battle Trance and Modern Body Wisdom
dek: You know the tight coil wrapped deep in your diaphragm, the one that whispers when anger rises—not as failure, but as urgent messenger. Celtic warriors entered a battle trance, an ancient somatic state of fierce survival and connection to the land and body. Today, your fury still speaks in muscle and nerve, and these older patterns offer a radical map for reclaiming your body’s wisdom.
bodyMarkdown:
You know that thick tension wrapped tight across your diaphragm, right beneath your sternum? It’s a clenched coil, almost a muscle memory you’ve never consciously learned but have carried like a secret manifest in your chest for years. That tight, weighty grip isn’t just anxiety or stress—it’s your anger speaking in body’s language, ancient and unfiltered. The anger pressing there twists and knots your diaphragm, narrowing your breath until it feels shallow, like the air itself is a currency you can’t afford in full measure. Your jaw clenches alongside it—the masseter muscle rigid, the temporomandibular joint taut, refusing to let go. This isn’t just a modern-day flare-up of emotion; it’s an echo from a time before words made sense, when the body led the way with instinct and survival.

This is the Celtic battle trance incarnate—a biological state honed over centuries when anger wasn’t so much a feeling as it was a fierce, embodied readiness to meet harm. Warriors entering battle entered this trance not by choice but by necessity. Their bodies became armored chambers of raw power and focus: the diaphragm locked to control breath, the jaw clenched to steady nerves, the trapezius and levator scapulae stiffened against the weight of imminent threat. The battle trance is a living, breathing manifestation of those historical moments when your forbearers had to become wolves in a human skin, sensing every shift in the air, every snarl in the distance.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work, reminds us that trauma is stored deep in the body. The fight, flight, or freeze responses that our ancestors relied on are not just past impulses but present realities, embedded in the fascia and nerve pathways below conscious thought. When your diaphragm tightens in a moment of anger, it is not a defect or failure—it is a signal from biology risking survival, insisting, “Here is danger. Prepare.” Your body does not distinguish a threat of physical harm from an emotional one; it only knows to summon its ancient wisdom.

The Celtic warriors’ battle trance offers us a vivid somatic blueprint. Imagine a warrior standing at dawn in a fogged valley, muscles coiled for violence yet strangely still. Their diaphragm is arrested mid-breath, a throttle valve locking down to preserve precious oxygen while the jaw anchors resolve, clenching so tightly that the teeth press like stones against muscles that will never release until the danger passes. This is not an emotional breakdown; it is embodied focus beyond the limits of ordinary consciousness. The bones, the muscles, the fascia all align in this trance, their tension a crucible for both rage and control.

Today, your anger’s familiar visit replicates this ancient pattern. The muscles around your jaw and across your throat tighten so rigidly you taste metal, your breath jolts shallowly like a frightened animal’s, and your diaphragm knots with such hardness it feels as if you will crack in two. This is your biological heritage, your nervous system draped in the armor of those warriors who demanded raw survival through sheer will and tension.

There’s a somatic irony here: the body’s attempt to secure safety through contraction often leaves you more at risk of fracture—the trapezius and levator scapulae held rigid for too long start burning with lactic acid; the jaw’s clenched masseter may lead to persistent headaches or TMJ syndrome; the diaphragm’s lockdown starves the brain of oxygen, dimming clarity in the very moments you need it most.

This is why the somatic approach to anger refuses the usual narratives of repression or uncontrolled outbursts. Instead, it respects the body’s logic. Anger arises not because you failed in calmness but because your nervous system is responding to perceived danger in the only way it knows—through muscle tension and breath control. The challenge isn’t to shame the anger but to recognize its shape in the body and work with it—softening the clench of the jaw, releasing the diaphragm from its rigid coil, opening a breath pathway so acute in the Celtic warriors that it carried them through battle and beyond.

Herbal allies—serious, earthy medicines like [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20)—serve as gentle emissaries to the nervous system in these moments. The bitter floral note of chamomile dilates the diaphragm subtly, allowing the breath to soften without forcing it, while its anti-inflammatory properties ease the spasms woven through muscle and fascia. Chamomile’s scent itself, steeped hot in porcelain, seems to melt the stone grip of the masseter and levator scapulae, coaxing out a release that modern bodies crave after centuries of inherited tension.

There is no quick fix here. Just as the Celtic battle trance wasn’t a uniform experience—sometimes raging, sometimes silent and watchful—your anger’s bodily signals change shape daily, hour by hour. It might come roaring through your psoas and trapezius when you’re cornered by injustice or simmer quietly in the tight wrap of your diaphragm late at night, made worse by the sharp cold of an October rain seeping into your bones. The body remembers the battlefields and the blood-soaked moors, and it still calls you back to that primal aliveness, that urgent story survival always gestures toward.

To meet this anger, you need to become fluent in the language of the jaw’s clench and the breath’s arrest. The masseter, when clenched, is not a villain; it is an ancient sentinel shouting warnings down nerve highways you barely access. The diaphragm’s tight coil is not a malfunction but a signal flare; by working somatically—sensing into those muscles, resourcing with breathwork and plant allies—you begin to translate this archaic fight-for-life code into something you can live with, something that no longer threatens your peace but protects your boundaries.

In those deep, early morning moments when the world outside your window is still a damp cold and your breath is short and shaky, remember that this tight diaphragm is the living past in your body. The battle trance is not gone; it waits to be understood, respected, and, when safe, unraveled. Each slow sip of chamomile tea is a small ritual of kindness to the muscle fibers, a balm for the fascia that remembers the war.

You carry this legacy everywhere—in your scowl, in the way your neck stiffens when you listen to stories of injustice, in the stubborn knotted hardness across your ribs. It is a fierce, vital, embodied inheritance. The modern body wisdom does not ask you to shed it but to honor it. To trace the map in your own flesh and learn, at last, to breathe through the rage with intention.

You’ve settled into the dimming hour between late afternoon and early evening, that spot when light softens but your body still hums with the after-effects of the day. The office chair’s mesh digs into your sacrum, a subtle irritation, and that familiar tightness has creepily nestled between your shoulder blades—levators scapulae, one on each side, like vigilant sentries. You’d swear they’re gripping your neck base, pulling your head sideways without your consent. That’s no surprise. These muscles carry history, and not just your own. Beneath the skin and muscle, within the glistening lattice of fascia, lies a record of inherited tension—what Resmaa Menakem calls the "white body" trauma held in flesh and bone, in a language without words but rich with sensation and memory.

## The Levator Scapulae and Your Ancestors’ Grip  
Let’s first acknowledge the levator scapulae with the proper respect it demands. This slender muscle runs from the transverse processes of the upper cervical vertebrae down to the upper corner of the scapula. When your neck tilts and rotates involuntarily—especially after a triggering comment or a moment when you had to hold anger tight and silent—those fascia sheets that enfold this muscle knit ever more densely. The fascia doesn’t just respond to current physical conditions; it responds to the emotional ones too. It thickens, it stiffens. It archives the weight of defensive postures adopted in moments of threat, moments like the ancient Celtic battle trance, those periods of immobilized rage born to survive violence. This isn’t metaphor. It’s fascial biology.

You might notice this the night you wake with a sharp jolt, your scalp slightly clammy from the damp chill slipping through windows that won’t quite close against the autumn dusk. That sensation of your neck caught in a vise, unable to turn toward safety or comfort—that is the levator scapulae’s tight grip, influenced by locked-down fight or flight energy stored not just in your brain but in the soft, collagenous tissues that drape over your skeleton. Resmaa Menakem’s work reminds us that trauma doesn’t only lodge in consciousness or psyche; trauma inscribes itself into the body’s cells and tissues with a stubborn physicality, particularly in areas designed to alert and protect. The levator scapulae is one of these sentient tissues, and sometimes, without conscious awareness, it calls forth that old Celtic defense—the battle trance.

## Psoas: The Body’s Ancient Sentinel  
Behind the hard shell of the abdominal wall, hidden beneath the intestines, sits the psoas major. When Pema Chödrön speaks about "sitting with discomfort," I often think of this muscle because the psoas does not separate easily between physical and emotional tension. It’s often dubbed the "fight or flight muscle," and with good reason. The psoas anchors your diaphragm to your lumbar spine, raising the ribs in breath, sending quick sharp ticks into the lower back and pelvis, and engaging your hips in readiness to flee or fight. When metaphors about gripping memories get tossed around casually, I think of the psoas’s contractile fibers holding tight to fears you can’t name, histories your body carries but your mind can’t access. This is where trauma—from your own life or your ancestral lineage—sets deeper roots.

If you press your fingers gently into the lower belly just lateral to your navel, that small hollow at the juncture of lumbar vertebrae and pelvis, you are close to the psoas. This muscle, wrapped in its own fascial sheath, does more than flex the hip; it holds onto tension long after the originating event has passed. Consider the old Celtic warriors, caught in the paralyzing grip of a battle trance when their nervous systems froze in the face of annihilation. Their psoas muscles clenched tight in suspended action, like a coiled serpent unable to strike or retreat, muscles locked as the fascia stiffened to preserve their survival. Today, in the quiet moments where your body resists movement, or where anxiety roots low into your abdomen, your psoas echoes that immense legacy.

These fascial layers don’t merely passively surround muscle fibers; they actively remember. The fascia is rich with sensory nerves—free nerve endings that tell the nervous system about tension, stretch, and compression. When trauma is encoded, the fascial glue stiffens, losing pliability and imprinting a pattern of contraction that repeats involuntarily. The tension in your psoas can, in a very real sense, be a whisper from the past—celestial memories of battle readiness inscribed in fascia, now misfiring as chronic tension, deep pelvic tightness, low back pain.

## The Slow Work of Unbinding Fascia  
Unbinding these deep stores of tension calls for more than stretching or superficial massage. This is where the somatic teachings of Robert Augustus Masters and Resmaa Menakem converge with traditional body wisdom. The fascia responds best to slow, informed movement—gentle traction, weight shifts, holding an edge without rushing into release. Think less about flexing for performance and more about inviting the fascia to soften, to hydrate, to reorient. It takes time and patience because the fascia remembers all trauma not as story but as sensation, often refusing to relax until it trusts that safety exists now.

Herbal allies support this tender work. Ashwagandha, rooted in Ayurvedic medicine for millennia, offers a potent tonic effect on the nervous system and musculoskeletal tension. The standardized extracts, like [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20), activate the parasympathetic nervous system, helping downregulate the relentless fight or flight signals still echoing in your levator scapulae or psoas. Taken regularly, this adaptogen soothes fascia, softens the rigid grip of old trauma, and invites a greater sense of fluid embodiment rather than stuck contraction.

Notice the shifts in your fascial webs on a day with rain pressing cold and damp into the air. Fascia tightens in response to humidity, and old injuries or ancestral patterns surge to the surface, demanding acknowledgment. When you tilt your head and feel the levator scapulae engage, or when you draw breath and feel the psoas’s stubborn clutch near your lumbar spine, try moving with gentleness. Become your own body’s historian, tracing the subtle moments when these muscles speak their ancestral pain—a pain no longer fierce defense but a whispered plea for release, for safety, for presence in a body that finally relaxes.

The modern body does not suffer alone; it carries the weight of a tribe’s ancient scripting in every twist and contraction. What you feel is not just consequence but inheritance. And in learning to listen—not just to your anger, but to the quiet pulsations in your fascia, your levator scapulae, your psoas—you begin the slow, patient unraveling of battle trance into living, breathing body wisdom.

You are here, somewhere between the remnant echoes of a Celtic war cry and the steady pulse of your own nervous system. That ancient battle trance—where the muscles froze, fists clenched like roots gripping earth in a storm, the breath held somewhere beneath your ribs—is not an archaic myth sealed safely behind history’s glass. It’s inside you, coiled beneath your skin, murmuring in the taut masseter muscle, and veiled in the sticky tension of your trapezius. This is the place where your body has learned to armor itself against perceived threat, often without your conscious consent. The good news—the revolutionary news framed in the language of contemporary somatic wisdom—is that you can unlearn this. The body remembers, yes, but it also can come back to life in new ways.

## The Nervous System’s Secret Language

Your nervous system is the great translator of these ancient battle rhythms. It’s what keeps your psoas muscle clenched beneath the belly or negotiates the catch in your diaphragm when you dare to let anger move into softness. Dr. Gabor Maté reminds us that trauma and tension are often trapped somatically as survival strategies that don’t automatically dissolve after danger passes. The nervous system lives in what Robert Augustus Masters calls “the tension zone”—not fully relaxed, not fully activated, but suspended somewhere in the middle, waiting and watching.

Imagine a cool, misty morning in the Scottish Highlands where the wind drifts low and smells faintly of heather and wet stone. This is what your parasympathetic nervous system craves: slow, steady signals that cannot be ignored. Because your body cannot untangle from the battle trance by sheer force of will. When you try, the trapezius tightens, the levator scapulae knots between the shoulders, and your breath shrinks like a frightened bird into the chest. Instead, the nervous system asks for language in sensations, rhythms, and pauses.

Somatic practitioners like Resmaa Menakem edge us toward a tangible way out of this tension—a recommended focus on “body-centered therapies” that aren’t about thinking your way out but feeling your way through. You cannot outrun the history in your fascia any more than you can outrun your own shadow. You must meet it where it lives.

## Embodied Practice: Releasing the Battle Trance

Here is a short embodied practice to help coax the nervous system out of its ancient armor. You don’t need a yoga mat or a quiet room—just the chair you’re sitting in or the floor beneath your feet.

Sit upright, feeling the solid weight of your sit bones anchoring you to the chair. Notice the subtle hum of tension in your jaw. Let your lips part slightly, just enough to break the seal of your teeth. Feel the masseter muscle—the thick band of tissue at the hinge of your jaw—and consciously invite it to soften.

Now, bring your attention to your diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs. Take a slow, deliberate breath in through your nose, feeling your belly rise slightly. Hold for a moment—your system in quiet readiness. Then release breath gently but fully through your parted lips, imagining the uncoil of your masseter, the unclenching of your jaw. Repeat this breathing cycle five times.

As you breathe out, invite the shoulders to roll backward once with a long, liquid motion—palms open, facing forward. Allow the trapezius and levator scapulae to soften as if you’re melting into a warm amber glow. With each exhale, imagine the stored warrior’s tension flowing out through your fingertips, the base of your skull, the back of your neck. Your diaphragm opens like a slowly unfolding fern frond, reclaiming space beneath your ribs to breathe fully. This is a slow unraveling, a releasing only possible when you stop resisting and start sensing.

## Integration with Modern Body Wisdom

The Celtic warrior’s trance was effective in its context: a sudden, programmatic response to immediate peril. Today, that same nervous system still conjures that freeze or fight response in moments of emotional threat—when a raised voice mirrors a battlefield, when the white noise of social media feels like encroaching armies.

Here is where the work is not only release but integration. It is a somatic dialogue, not a battle. The levator scapulae and trapezius are no longer foes to be defeated but messengers bringing ancient signals into modern attention. Teachers like Pema Chödrön and Thich Nhat Hanh guide this with mindfulness of sensation and breath—not suppression. Resmaa Menakem underscores this with racialized somatic trauma, showing that the muscle memory of fear speaks across generations.

The body isn’t just the battleground; it’s the home. Every fiber of fascia, every slow calcium release in the muscle cells, can soften with patience and respect to new signals that say, “You are safe now.” Using the subtle support of herbs—a daily cup of nettle tea, richly mineral and bright—grounds this practice in the physical and energetic reality of your body. The nettle’s iron and silica nourish the blood and connective tissue where tension lodges. [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a potent ally here.

This is not about fixing or medicating but about nurturing the body’s own wisdom to unfold old trauma, coax taut tendons into flex, and bring the diaphragm fully into its sovereign role as breath’s origin and bellows.

Let this practice become a tonic, part of your daily ritual—a moment where you honor the body’s tired battle lines, where you recognize tension as a form of communication, and where you choose, slowly, to let the old trance transform.

## FAQ

**Q: What exactly is the “battle trance,” and how does it show up in my body today?**  
**A:** The battle trance is a term describing the state your nervous system shifts into during life-threatening situations—muscles stiffen, the breath shortens, and the body becomes wired for fight, flight, or freeze. Today, it can show up as chronic jaw clenching, a tight neck, shallow breathing, or a persistent “on edge” feeling even in situations that aren’t dangerous. It’s not a failure; it’s biology adapting to past threats.

**Q: Can I reverse these tensions without professional bodywork?**  
**A:** Yes, you can begin with slow, attentive breathing and somatic awareness practices that help your nervous system re-learn safety. However, deep release may require skilled hands or guided somatic therapy, especially if trauma is involved. That said, daily rituals like consciously softening the jaw and shoulders, combined with gentle herbal support, are vital and effective first steps.

**Q: Why nettle tea? How does it really help with this process?**  
**A:** Nettle is a powerhouse herb rich in minerals such as iron, calcium, and silica, supporting the health of your blood and connective tissue—the very tissues where chronic tension often locks in. It’s not a sedative but a nutritive tonic that helps your body maintain resilience and repair, making it a profound ally when you are working to soften battle-damaged muscle and fascia.

**Q: I feel discomfort and even emotional waves when I try to release tension. Is that normal?**  
**A:** Absolutely. When you start to untangle layers of chronic tension, the nervous system may flare up as these “defenses” drop. You might feel bursts of anger, sadness, or even numbness temporarily. This is your body and psyche communicating. It’s essential to approach these feelings with kindness and patience, allowing them to move without judgment.

**Q: How can I sustain this practice on days when anger feels overwhelming or flooding?**  
**A:** When anger surges, your nervous system is asking for you to witness without reacting immediately. Grounding yourself with breath, a simple gesture like rolling your shoulders, or making a nettle tea can anchor you. Remember the body knows how to find peace—it just needs your permission and space to do so, one breath at a time.
